coredata.dc:title: Nucleus-targeting pH-Responsive carbon dots for fast nucleus pH detection
coredata.dc:creator: Durrani, Samran
Yang, Zihuayuan
Zhang, Jie
Wang, Zihao
Wang, Haiyan
Durrani, Faran
Wu, Fu-Gen
Lin, Fengming
coredata.dc:description: Nucleus pH is closely linked to many diseases such as aging, heart disease, skeletal myopathies, cancer, Alzheimer's disease, etc. Nevertheless, fluorescent sensors that can directly monitor nucleus pH changes have not yet been reported. Here, we first reported a green emissive carbon dots (CDs) for nucleus pH detection in living cells. CDs can selectively target nucleus with high accumulation at nucleolus due to their high affinity towards RNA once entering cells by lipid raft mediated endocytosis. Without washing, CDs at 5 μg/mL was enough to lighten nucleus within 10 min with the fluorescence on ever after 24 h incubation, achieving fast, wash-free, and long-term nucleus/nucleolus imaging. Meanwhile, the luminescent intensity of CDs was reduced gradually when pH changed continuously from 1 to 12, showing a pH-responsive fluorescence property with two linear ranges of pH 2–7 and pH 7–12. With their nucleus-targeting ability and pH-dependent photoluminescent property, CDs was successfully leveraged for nucleus pH detection in A549 cells and for in vivo pH sensing in zebra fish. CDs present a promising and powerful fluorescent sensor for nucleus imaging and nucleus pH sensing in living cells on the way to understand nucleus-related biological events.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nucleus pH detection
Carbon quantum dots
Biosensor
Nucleus tracking
Long-rang pH sensor
In-vivo pH sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CDs target nucleus with high accumulation at nucleolus
CDs for fast, wash-free, and long-term nucleus/nucleolus imaging
The pH-responsive fluorescence property of CDs
In vivo pH detection using CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Confocal fluorescence images of A549, HepG2, L02, and L929 cells co-stained with CDs and Hoechst 33,342. (B) Confocal images of A549 labeled by CDs after treatment with DNase or RNase.

Confocal fluorescence images of A549 cells incubated with CDs (A) with and without washing steps for different time periods (B) Confocal images of A549 cells stained with CDs at different concentrations (1, 5, 10, 20, 50 or 100 μg/mL). (C) Confocal images of A549 cells treated with 10 μg/mL CDs for different time periods. (D) Effects of staining time on the fluorescence intensity of A549 cells stained by CDs.

(A) CLSM images and (B) fluorescence intensity of A549 cells under different conditions. Control: Cells without CDs; 37 °C: Cells were stained with 10 μg/mL CDs for 30 min at 37 °C; 4 °C, NaN3, CPZ, genistein, amiloride, or MβCD: cells were first treated at 4 °C for 30 min or incubated with NaN3, CPZ, genistein, amiloride, or MβCD for 2 h, and then labeled with CDs (10 μg/mL). (C) Schematic design of the cellular uptake mechanism of CDs.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs solutions with different pH under 440 nm excitation. (B) Plot of pH versus PL intensity ratio (F1–F)/F1 of CDs with different pH from 2 to 12. (λex = 440 nm and λem = 510 nm) F1 and F refers to the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs at 510 nm in a solution with pH 1 and other pH, respectively. (C) pH reversibility of CDs between pH 1 and 12.

Nucleus pH determination in A549 cells using CDs. (A) CLSM images of A549 incubated with 10 μg/mL CDs at pH 3,7, or 9. (B) The corresponding cellular fluorescence intensity of all samples in (A) were determined by flow cytometry analysis. (C) The corresponding statistics of the flow cytometric results in (B).
CLSM images of zebra fish first incubated with different pH values (pH 3,7, or 9), and then stained with 100 μg/mL CDs.
Schematic diagram illustrating the application s of CDs for long range pH detection, nucleus pH sensing, and in-vivo pH monitoring.

head.ce:title: Nucleus-targeting pH-Responsive carbon dots for fast nucleus pH detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Botany University of Science and Technology Bannu, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Botany University of Science and Technology Bannu

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Botany University of Science and Technology Bannu, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Samran Durrani and Zihuayuan Yang contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nucleus pH is closely linked to many diseases such as aging, heart disease, skeletal myopathies, cancer, Alzheimer's disease, etc. Nevertheless, fluorescent sensors that can directly monitor nucleus pH changes have not yet been reported. Here, we first reported a green emissive carbon dots (CDs) for nucleus pH detection in living cells. CDs can selectively target nucleus with high accumulation at nucleolus due to their high affinity towards RNA once entering cells by lipid raft mediated endocytosis. Without washing, CDs at 5 μg/mL was enough to lighten nucleus within 10 min with the fluorescence on ever after 24 h incubation, achieving fast, wash-free, and long-term nucleus/nucleolus imaging. Meanwhile, the luminescent intensity of CDs was reduced gradually when pH changed continuously from 1 to 12, showing a pH-responsive fluorescence property with two linear ranges of pH 2–7 and pH 7–12. With their nucleus-targeting ability and pH-dependent photoluminescent property, CDs was successfully leveraged for nucleus pH detection in A549 cells and for in vivo pH sensing in zebra fish. CDs present a promising and powerful fluorescent sensor for nucleus imaging and nucleus pH sensing in living cells on the way to understand nucleus-related biological events.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a burgeoning class of carbon-derived fluorescent nanomaterials that have gained considerable interest in recent years [1–8]. Due to their merits in excellent luminescence, great biocompatibility, high stability, and low cost, CDs show wide utilizations in fluorescent nanoprobes [9–16], bioimaging [17–25], optoelectronic devices [26–28], photo-catalysis [23,29–31], etc. In particular, CDs are promising fluorescent nanosensors for the measurement of biological compounds. In the past decade, tremendous research efforts have been made to develop novel CD-based fluorescent sensors for the detection and quantification of biologically important molecules that are crucial for human health, such as cell parameters (pH and temperature), small molecules (biothiols, amino acids, reactive oxygen species, and vitamins), and biomacromolecules (proteins, RNA, and DNA) [32]. Nevertheless, most CDs for intracellular detection are achieved in the whole cells, rather than in subcellular compartments. To precisely realize subcellular detection is of great importance, given the levels of biological compounds and parameters can vary among subcellular organelles.

pH of subcellular compartments are significantly different. The normal pH of mitochondria, nucleus, endoplasmic reticulum, cytosol, early endosome, late endosome, and lysosome is 8, 7.2, 7.2, 7.2, 6.3, 5.5, and 4.7, respectively [33,34]. In eukaryotic cells, the establishment of a unique pH is essential for the distinct function of individual organelles [35]. Nucleus maintains neutral pH ∼7.2 under physiological conditions [2]. Abnormal nucleus pH leads to the abnormalities in the nuclear morphology, which have been related to a plethora of diseases, including aging [11], heart disease [12], skeletal myopathies [13], cancer [14], Alzheimer's disease [15], and so on. Thus, being able to probe nucleus pH is key to understand nucleus function under both physiological and pathological conditions [11]. Nevertheless, the pH detection in nucleus is more challenge than other subcellular organelles, due to that the nuclear pores are too small for the entry of probes with large sizes. To our best knowledge, no techniques is available for nucleus pH detection.

CDs-based fluorescent pH probes have been developed to determine organelle pH in living cells. Most CD-originated pH sensors available detect pH in vitro or in the whole cell rather than in subcellular compartments [36–38]. Only several organelle-targeting CDs with intrinsic pH-responsive property have been constructed for monitoring pH in lysosome [39–43] and ER [40]. Ye et al. have fabricated two-photon fluorescent CDs by hydrothermal approach, which have been leveraged as a fluorescent sensor for lysosomal pH [41]. Zhang et al. reported functional CDs with emerald emission and lysosome-targeting ability, achieving the pH detection in lysosome in vitro with high sensitivity and selectivity [39]. Shuang et al. have applied laurylamine-functionalized CDs and amine-functionalized CDs to trace the pH changes in lysosome and endoplasmic reticulum respectively [40]. However, no CD-based pH fluorescence probes in nucleus have been reported, though CDs have been demonstrated to be powerful in nucleus/nucleolus imaging because of their ultrasmall sizes [44–47].

In this study, green-emitting CDs constructed by hydrothermal treatment of Miswak (Mis) and m-phenylenediamine (mPD) as reported previously [48] was harnessed for nucleus-imaging, pH monitoring, and nucleus pH detection (Scheme 1 ). CDs can enter the cells via lipid raft mediated endocytosis and reach nucleus very quickly, showing higher affinity to RNA than DNA. CDs at low concentrations were applied for fast and long-term nucleus labeling without washing. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was gradually reduced when pH was changed from 1 to 12, featuring pH-responsive fluorescence characteristic. Based on their nucleus-oriented capability and pH-dependent fluorescent characteristic, CDs were utilized to track pH changes in nucleus in living cells, acting as the first-reported fluorescent sensor for nucleus pH, as far as we know. Also, the in vivo pH monitoring with CDs was performed in zebra fish.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs target nucleus with high accumulation at nucleolus
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized from Miswak roots and mPD using hydrothermal method as described in our previous study [48]. Topographical characterization of synthesized CDs by transmission electron microscope (TEM) was performed to demonstrate the successful preparation of CDs (Fig. S1). Green fluorescence was found in nucleus of A549 cells treated with green-emitting CDs, demonstrating that CDs was selectively localized at nucleus after entering the cells (Fig. 1 A). Particularly, much brighter green fluorescence was found in nucleolus than the other areas of nucleus, showing that CDs accumulated highly in nucleolus. To verify the intrinsic nucleus-targeting ability of CDs, A549 cells were costained with CDs and commercially nuclear dye Hoechst 33,342. Hoechst 33,342 stained the whole nucleus averagely (Fig. 1A), which was overlapped well with the green fluorescence of CDs, demonstrating the specific nucleus-targeting property of CDs. Moreover, the nucleus-targeting ability of CDs was also observed in other animal cells like HepG2, L02, and L929 (Fig. 1A).

To investigate the mechanism of selective staining of nucleus by CDs, ribonuclease DNase hydrolyzing DNA and RNase hydrolyzing RNA and digestion experiments were performed using A549 cells (Fig. 1B). CDs was located at the nucleoli in DNase-treated A549 cells, while evenly distributed in the whole nucleus in RNase-treated ones. This demonstrated that CDs displayed higher affinity towards RNA than DNA though CDs can bind to both, well explaining why CDs was highly accumulated in nucleoli given that it has long been known that most RNAs (majorly in the form of rRNA) in nucleus is distributed in nucleolus [46]. Moreover, in order to evaluate the safety of CDs, we assessed viabilities of live animal cells (A549) by MTT assays in the presence of CDs (Fig. S2). In our study, more than 90% of A549 cells were alive after treatment with CDs at concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 μg/mL. This indicates that CDs have excellent biosafety and are nontoxic at the concentration (10 μg/mL) used for cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs for fast, wash-free, and long-term nucleus/nucleolus imaging
ce:section.ce:para: We further explored the nucleus-staining conditions of CDs, including washing, concentration, and incubation time. First, we incubated A549 cells with CDs for different time periods and took CLSM images with or without a washing step (Fig. 2 A). The A549 cells without a washing step also showed strong and bright fluorescence in the nucleus, similar to that in A549 cells with a washing step, indicating no washing is required for the nucleus-staining of CDs, which is convenient and labor-saving. Therefore, no washing was performed for the nucleus-imaging of CDs in further study. Next, we evaluated the working concentrations of CDs for labeling nucleus from 1 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL (Fig. 2B). 10 μg/mL of CDs was enough to lighten nucleus, which is much lower than the working concentrations of other nucleus-imaging CDs reported in literature [47,49,50]. Meanwhile, the effect of incubation times of A549 cells with CDs was tested on the nucleus-imaging capability of CDs (Fig. 2C and D). CDs was distributed very quickly to nucleus. Green fluorescence was observed in the nucleus of A549 after 2-min incubation with CDs, and was bright enough to image nucleus at 10 min (Fig. 2C), which was super fast as compered to the incubation time (more than 0.5 h) used for other nucleus-imaging CDs reported in recent years [47,49–51]. The fluorescence intensity of A549 reached a plateau after 2-h incubation with CDs (Fig. 2D). Moreover, green fluorescence was still found in nucleus even at 24 h, showing that CDs can be utilized for long-term nucleus-labeling (Fig. 2C). These findings implicate that CDs are competent as a fluorescent probe for wash-free, fast, and long-term nucleus-imaging with a low working concentration.

Next, the cellular uptake pathways of CDs were determined. Commonly, the cellular uptake of nanomaterials are mainly affected by temperature and energy supply (inhibited by NaN3), which can block active transport like cell endocytosis. There are four major types of cell endocytosis: clathrin-mediated endocytosis (CME), caveolae-mediated endocytosis (CvME), micropinocytosis, and lipid raft-mediated endocytosis (LrME), which is inhibited by chlorpromazine (CPZ), genistein, 5-(N,Ndimethyl)-amiloride hydrochloride (amiloride), and methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD). The cellular localization of CDs in A549 cells treated with 4 °C and different inhibitors was visualized by CLSM (Fig. 3 A) and the corresponding fluorescence intensity was quantified via a flow cytometer (Fig. 3B). As compared to the fluorescence in A549 cells incubated with only CDs at 37 °C, the fluorescence in the 4 °C group and MβCD-group was declined by ∼30% and ∼55%, respectively (Fig. 3A and B). Meanwhile, the fluorescence in the NaN3-, CPZ-, genistein-, and amiloride-treated cells was decreased by 0%, 10%, 15%, and 15% respectively. These observations elucidated that the cellular entrance of CDs depends on energy-dependent transport and LrME. Obviously, CDs entered cells via lipid raft mediated endocytosis to target selectively in nucleus (Fig. 3C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pH-responsive fluorescence property of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of CDs in response to pH changes was determined. As pH varied continuously from 1 to 12, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 510 nm was decreased gradually (Fig. 4 A). The experimental linear regression equation plot was made by plotting the pH values versus the fluorescence intensity ratio of (F1–F)/F1 where F1 and F refers to the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs at 510 nm in a solution with pH 1 and other pH values, respectively (Fig. 4B). The fluorescence intensity ratio (F1–F)/F1 exhibited good linearity with pH variation in the range of pH 2–7 and pH 7–12, respectively. The linear range is much broader than the existing pH probes in the literature and fulfills the demands for intracellular pH monitoring (Table S1) [39–43,52–56]. Moreover, the relative fluorescence intensity of CDs presented an excellent reversibility between pH 1 and pH 12 (Fig. 4C), indicating that CDs is capable of reversibly monitoring pH, which is desirable considering the reversibility of pH changes in biological environments. Of note, the pH sensing ability of CDs is possibly ascribed to protonation-deprotonation of the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs [67–69] [48]. Nevertheless, the pH sensing mechanism of CDs is not fully understood, awaiting further investigation.

The intrinsic nucleus-targeting ability and pH-dependent fluorescence property of CDs indicate that CDs might be applied to monitor nucleus pH. To this end, A549 cells with Ph 3, 7, and 9 were labeled with CDs and imaged under CLSM (Fig. 5 A). When pH was changed from 3 to 9, the green fluorescence in the nucleus A549 was gradually decreased (Fig. 5A). In addition to CLSM, CDs can also determine pH quantitatively in nucleus using flow cytometry (Fig. 5B and C). The fluorescence intensity of A549 cells in the presence of CDs was reduced with the increased pH, in agreement with the confocal imaging data (Fig. 5A). These results demonstrated CDs can be deployed to image and sense nucleus pH in living cells. As far as we know, CDs present the first fluorescent sensor for nucleus pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo pH detection using CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Given that CDs can be applied for the sensing of nucleus pH, we are wondering whether CDs could be exploited for assessing pH in vivo. To this end, zebrafishes with different pH including 3, 7, or 9 were incubated with 100 μg/mL CDs. The fluorescence intensity of CDs in zebrafish was declined steadily along with the increase of pH (Fig. 6 ). The above data reveals that CDs can be leveraged for the semiquantitative tracking of pH in vivo, as demonstrated in zebrafish. As found in our previous research, CDs exhibited good anti-interference ability to varied biological ions and molecules including Ca2+, K+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, glutathione (GSH), melamine (Mel), human serum albumin (HSA), glycine (Gly), and dopamine (DA), and high photostability [48], which is important for practical applications given that the living tissue or cell environment is very complex.

In summary, green-emitting CDs were demonstrated to be a potent probe for nucleus-imaging, pH monitoring, and nucleus pH detection. CDs was quickly internalized by the cells via lipid raft mediated endocytosis and localized specifically at nucleus, displaying higher affinity to RNA than DNA. Because of this, CDs at low concentrations were exploited for fast and long-term nucleus imaging with no washing step. Furthermore, the green fluorescence intensity of CDs was reduced steadily when pH changed continuously from 1 to 12, featuring a pH-responsive fluorescence property. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F1–F)/F1 exhibited good linearity with pH variation in the range of pH 2–7 and pH 7–12, respectively. On the basis of their nucleus-targeting ability and pH-dependent photoluminescent property, CDs was successfully deployed for nucleus pH monitoring in A549 cells and for in vivo pH sensing in zebrafish. It is the first time to develop fluorescent nanoprobes that can detect nucleus pH in living cells, as far as we know. We believe CDs can serve as a potent fluorescent sensor for nucleus imaging and nucleus pH sensing in the understanding of nucleus-associated physiological processes and pathological events.

Samran Durrani: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft and Project administration. Zihuayuan Yang: Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Validation. Jie Zhang: Validation. Zihao Wang: Validation. Haiyan Wang: Validation. Faran Durrani: Validation. Fu-Gen Wu: Validation. Fengming Lin: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Visualization, Writing – original draft and Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Resources, Methodology and Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32170072), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, and Research Innovation Program of International Graduate Students of Southeast University (SEU).
